draft.introduction: Chapter 56 opens with one of the most famous paradoxes in world philosophy: "Those who know do not speak; those who speak do not know." It describes the sage's state of mystical union with all things.

translation.text: Those who know do not speak. Those who speak do not know. Block the openings, shut the doors, blunt the sharpness, untangle the knots, soften the glare, settle with the dust. This is called the mysterious union. It cannot be approached or kept at a distance. It cannot be benefited or harmed. It cannot be honored or disgraced. Therefore it is the most precious thing under heaven.

translator_notes.note: The "mysterious union" (xuan tong) describes a state beyond all dualities—closeness and distance, benefit and harm, honor and disgrace. This concept influenced Chan (Zen) Buddhism's emphasis on non-dual awareness and its distrust of verbal formulations of truth.
